templates an eventual return to his forfeited acres; the pessimism is sharp,
clear, and uncomprising. Rosemary Neilsen (2) finds political misgivings
in Е.1., a challenge to Octavian' s land policy, an appeal for the end
of fratricide and cruel expropriation. To win the divine status Octavian
must review his political programme. P.L. Smith (3) visualizes the avena
as an oatstraw monaulos, a primitive country instrument, which intensifies
the archetypal setting for the Eclogues and symbolizes the process of
pastoral composition. Yebra (4) deals with the firstlines of E. 1. Robertson
(5) studies E. 2 for adaptations, omissions and accretions of Theocritus
Idylls 3 and 11, and also finds affinities between Vergil's poetic lusus
and Gallus' poetic genre. Braun' s study (6) of the musical competition
in E. 3 and 7 marks no significant advances. Eleanor Leach (7) explores
the Platonic background ofthe Wunderkind in E. 4 and argues that allegorized myth is the spirit behind an idealistic, philosophical vision of human
government. Her awareness of the poem as a new image of the heroic
is likely to be influential in subsequent criticism. Stephen Waite's study
(8) of the contest in E. 7 is brief but enlightening. Bowersock's date
(10) has important implications for E. 8 and for the remainder of the
bucolic corpus. Carrière (12) studies V.'s lyrical manner in the bucolic
poems, a blend of description and contemplation in the manner of Keats,
Goethe, Lamartine etc. Brooks Otis (17) reviews his own interpretation
of the Eclogues in his monumental Virgil: A Study in Civilized Poetry
(Oxford, 1963). He finds that Klingner's literary sensitivity and sound
historical sense have brought readers perceptibly nearer to what Vergil
actually intended when he composed his bucolic poems. Otis' apologetic
review is required reading for students, and an incentive to closer scrutiny
of Klinger's Virgil (1967).

Wilkinson's study of The Georgics of Virgil (Cambridge, 1969)
has aroused dormant interest for Vergil's capo lavoro, two thousand
years old. Escanciano's version and notes are often obvious, sometimes
perceptive (19, 20). Borzsák (22) supplies new insights on the Libyan
and Scythian digressions in G. 3. His comparative study of arguments
and techniques in Poseidonius are important and profitable. Capponi (23)
struggles with the unidentifiable acalanthis which may perhaps be linked,
by association, with alcyon (cf. G. 2, 328). Coleiro's article (25) explores
the double allegory underlying the community of bees and the Aristaeus
episode in G. 4, and raises the ghost of C. Cornelius Gallus again. For
Colleiro there are positive links between Aristaeus and the elegist, for
both share guilt, against Eurydice or Augustus, both suffer loss, bees
and political amicitia, and the shepherd's pardon was pattern for Gallus
had he been contrite to Augustus and appealed for mercy. Colleiro argues,
somewhat desperately, that Aeneid 1,2, and 4 inspired comparable passages and expressions in G. 4, thereby implying that the Aristaeus episode
was (as Servius remarked, and Büchner and Richter have accepted) inserted after the firstpublication of the Georgics in 29; Gallus' suicide in
26 ВС therefore induced Vergil to issue a second version of the fourth
Geòrgie. Grimm's article (26) provides fresh evidence for Gallus' role
in the 30's. Wormell (27), with characteristic clarity, finds an artistic
role for the bees in the composition of the Georgics. In their tiny, not
wholly human way, they bridge the gap between the agricultural tragedy
of Book 3 and the human tragedy of Book 4. Fischer's dissertation (29)
offers important comment on the geographical digressions in the Georgics

and sets the practice in a wider context. MacKenzie's verse translation
(30), with contemporary etchings by Lambourne, has won the commendation of Wilkinson (CR 22 (1972) 107-8). McKay's lecture (31) marked
the bimillennium of Vergil's four poetic books on agriculture and explored
Vergil's successful marriage between technology and poetry along with
its overtones for our present environmental and cultural crisis. Otis (32)
provides a lengthy and provocative review of Wilkinson's The Georgics
ofVirgil (Cambridge 1969). Klinger, Otis, and Wilkinson provide a formidable triumvirate of modern scholarship on the Georgics. The late Adam
Parry's lecture (33) moves from G. 3, 49-71 to a wider study of poetic
invention and variety in connection with external phenomena in the Georgics. Parry concentrates on G. 4 where the train or "suite" of thought
and expression is most significantly evidenced. Pridik (34) has composed
a valuable study of structure which merits the closest attention from
scholars. Saint-Denis (36) uses Columella to explicate some Vergilian
cruces in G. 1 (56), 2 (9-37, and 3 (40-42). K.D. White (37), noted for
his remarkable study of Roman Farming (London & Ithaca, 1970), provides additional fodder for technical explication of the Georgics in his
bibliography.

templates an eventual return to his forfeited acres; the pessimism is sharp,
clear, and uncomprising. Rosemary Neilsen (2) finds political misgivings
in Е.1., a challenge to Octavian' s land policy, an appeal for the end
of fratricide and cruel expropriation. To win the divine status Octavian
must review his political programme. P.L. Smith (3) visualizes the avena
as an oatstraw monaulos, a primitive country instrument, which intensifies
the archetypal setting for the Eclogues and symbolizes the process of
pastoral composition. Yebra (4) deals with the firstlines of E. 1. Robertson
(5) studies E. 2 for adaptations, omissions and accretions of Theocritus
Idylls 3 and 11, and also finds affinities between Vergil's poetic lusus
and Gallus' poetic genre. Braun' s study (6) of the musical competition
in E. 3 and 7 marks no significant advances. Eleanor Leach (7) explores
the Platonic background ofthe Wunderkind in E. 4 and argues that allegor-
ized myth is the spirit behind an idealistic, philosophical vision of human
government. Her awareness of the poem as a new image of the heroic
is likely to be influential in subsequent criticism. Stephen Waite's study
(8) of the contest in E. 7 is brief but enlightening. Bowersock's date
(10) has important implications for E. 8 and for the remainder of the
bucolic corpus. Carrière (12) studies V.'s lyrical manner in the bucolic
poems, a blend of description and contemplation in the manner of Keats,
Goethe, Lamartine etc. Brooks Otis (17) reviews his own interpretation
of the Eclogues in his monumental Virgil: A Study in Civilized Poetry
(Oxford, 1963). He finds that Klingner's literary sensitivity and sound
historical sense have brought readers perceptibly nearer to what Vergil
actually intended when he composed his bucolic poems. Otis' apologetic
review is required reading for students, and an incentive to closer scrutiny

Wilkinson's study of The Georgics of Virgil (Cambridge, 1969)
has aroused dormant interest for Vergil's capo lavoro, two thousand
years old. Escanciano's version and notes are often obvious, sometimes
perceptive (19, 20). Borzsák (22) supplies new insights on the Libyan
and Scythian digressions in G. 3. His comparative study of arguments
and techniques in Poseidonius are important and profitable. Capponi (23)
struggles with the unidentifiable acalanthis which may perhaps be linked,
by association, with alcyon (cf. G. 2, 328). Coleiro's article (25) explores
the double allegory underlying the community of bees and the Aristaeus
episode in G. 4, and raises the ghost of C. Cornelius Gallus again. For
Colleiro there are positive links between Aristaeus and the elegist, for
both share guilt, against Eurydice or Augustus, both suffer loss, bees
and political amicitia, and the shepherd's pardon was pattern for Gallus
had he been contrite to Augustus and appealed for mercy. Colleiro argues,
somewhat desperately, that Aeneid 1,2, and 4 inspired comparable pass-
ages and expressions in G. 4, thereby implying that the Aristaeus episode
was (as Servius remarked, and Büchner and Richter have accepted) insert-
ed after the firstpublication of the Georgics in 29; Gallus' suicide in
26 ВС therefore induced Vergil to issue a second version of the fourth
Geòrgie. Grimm's article (26) provides fresh evidence for Gallus' role
in the 30's. Wormell (27), with characteristic clarity, finds an artistic
role for the bees in the composition of the Georgics. In their tiny, not
wholly human way, they bridge the gap between the agricultural tragedy
of Book 3 and the human tragedy of Book 4. Fischer's dissertation (29)
offers important comment on the geographical digressions in the Georgics

and sets the practice in a wider context. MacKenzie's verse translation
(30), with contemporary etchings by Lambourne, has won the commenda-
tion of Wilkinson (CR 22 (1972) 107-8). McKay's lecture (31) marked
the bimillennium of Vergil's four poetic books on agriculture and explored
Vergil's successful marriage between technology and poetry along with
its overtones for our present environmental and cultural crisis. Otis (32)
provides a lengthy and provocative review of Wilkinson's The Georgics
ofVirgil (Cambridge 1969). Klinger, Otis, and Wilkinson provide a formid-
able triumvirate of modern scholarship on the Georgics. The late Adam
Parry's lecture (33) moves from G. 3, 49-71 to a wider study of poetic
invention and variety in connection with external phenomena in the Geor-
gics. Parry concentrates on G. 4 where the train or "suite" of thought
and expression is most significantly evidenced. Pridik (34) has composed
a valuable study of structure which merits the closest attention from
scholars. Saint-Denis (36) uses Columella to explicate some Vergilian
cruces in G. 1 (56), 2 (9-37, and 3 (40-42). K.D. White (37), noted for
his remarkable study of Roman Farming (London & Ithaca, 1970), pro-
vides additional fodder for technical explication of the Georgics in his
bibliography.

Deryck Williams brings insight and a measure of excitement to practically
every page of his textbook. He carefully notes his departures from the
text of Mynors and Hirtzel, and generally convinces one of the wisdom
of his choice. Students and lecturers will welcome this first volume with
joy and gratitude. The commentary (154-517) is refreshingly modern, nicely attuned to structure, thematic language and poetics. Austin (39), with
customary authority, style, and a measure of polemic, provides the first
detailed English commentary on Aeneid I since R.S. Conway's edition
(1935). The text and commentary are exemplary, and offer a fine inducement to studious, pleasurable reading. Segal (40) interprets the Iopas
vignette and the elaborate cosmological song in its immediate and larger
context. He notes that the subject matter enlarges to cosmic proportions
the imagery of fireand darkness which pervades the close of Aeneid

The bard's song telescopes present and future, individual and cosmic
destinies, into a single moment of poetic vision. The alignment of human
events with universal processes for mutual illumination is perfectly evidenced in Iopas' song where the broader, Platonic mysteries, are glimpsed
in this prelude to human tragedy. Ferguson (42) offers further comment
on the controlling images of fire and wound inAen. 4. Only the hearth-fire,
token of domesticity and family life, is missing. The wound has two
tangential aspects: (1) disease (fire and poison), and (2) the hunt (hunter
and quarry). Bertman's article (46) explores the polarity of generations
in Aen. 5, where the generation gap is given symbolic, forceful exposition.
Opperman (47) and Somville (48) provide critical studies of the Palinurus
episode in Aen. 5. Both concentrate on the Somnus passage, the implications of the intrusion and the response of the helmsman. Stégen (49)
compares and contrasts the Vergilian boxing match with Homer, Iliad,
23, 653-75, the obvious model, but with important structural differences.
Barbu (50) deals with the hierarchy of human values and the law of universal necessity discernible in Aen. 6. Dupont-Neraudau (52) combine to
give some novel insights into the Marcellus episode. Rieks (53) and Rutledge (54) are complementary exercises; Rieks is concerned with the
heroic pomp at the close, Rutledge with the Daedalic gallery at the outset
of Aen. 6 Rutledge argues that the artist is a creature of marked insight,
with interpretative gifts which can impart order, direction and definition
to men's lives; Aeneas studies the panels with a sense of their importance.
The labyrinth suggests the ordeal and the triumph which await Aeneas
in the underworld. Sabbadini's (5) Aeneid 6, with commentary, is a welcome reprint and worthy of wider study. Rev. R.V. Schoder's pictorial
study (56) of the Campi Phlegraei repeats his earlier article in Romanitas
9 (1970) 187-98. He provides superb photographs of the area which made
a profound impression on the poet and on modern Vergilians who follow
in his steps. Solmsen (58) argues against the tripartite scheme of Aen.
6, detected by Norden, Frances Norwood, and Otis, as irrelevant and
inadequate. He underscores the overriding importance ofthe dichotomous
pattern. Impressed with Vergil's sympathy with the individual in his physi-

Catabasis of Heracles, favoured by Norden, as will-o'-the-wisp and undemonstrable as a source. Williams (60) comments on Vergil's use of
traditional material as background for what is special and personal to
himself and the poem: the shapes at the entrance, Charon's boat, and
the ghost of Palinurus. Charon's presence, the traditional boatman and
the river, helps to diminish the tension; the pictorial, mock-heroic writing
is an intermezzo to the pathos of Palinurus and Dido. Huxley (62) offers
a comic expansion ofAeneid 7, 107-34 in hexameters. Jung (63) provides
background to Vergil's use of Thybris, the Lydian stream. Merkelbach
(64) finds typical Roman epigrammatic formulae in the address to Caieta.
Her death marks the transition to the Roman half of the Aeneid. Wimmel
(65) explores bucolic themes and expressions which characterize the central books (5-8) of the epic, the Alexandrian books which separate the
Odyssey and
Iliad portions. The bucolic aspect of the middle books
helps to diminish the epic-heroic scale and portentous atmosphere; there
is instead, an easy familiarity, a world of dreams, delusions, and pastoral
and rural elements reminiscent of the Eclogues and Georgics. Wimmel's
brief study (21 pages) merits the closest attention by scholars. Tanner
(66) favours an allegorical interpretation of the last six books of the epic.
Convinced that the political and military events connected with Mutina,
Philippi, Perusia and Actium lurk beneath the poetic lines, Tanner finds
Cicero the pattern for Evander and Pallas, Catiline for Cacus, Maecenas
for Tarchon. Nisus and Euryalus reflect Hirtius and Pansa (a counteipart
discovered earlier in my Vergil*s Italy
p.45); Achates is a rather tightlipped reflection of Agrippa. Behind Drances there lies neither Cicero
nor Maecenas, whose names have been preferred earlier, but L. Munatius
Plancus. The impact of contemporary politics on the poet has rarely
been argued so vigorously. Latinus-Lepidus, Lavinia-Servilia, AmataServilia the Elder, mother of Brutus, will haunt the imagination far more
disturbingly than Octavian-Aeneas or Antony-Turnus. Binder (67) offers
the best available interpretation of Aen. 8 so far available. He provides
a grand explication de texte, a line-by-line analysis of the book, with
heavy emphasis (133 pages) on the shield. This model of detailed scholarship will do much to remedy popular disinterest in the last six books
of the epic. Galinsky (68) advances Plato, Euthyd. 297C, to illuminate
Aen. 8, 299-30. Plato's equation of the Hydra with cleverness and reason
brings Hercules into a philosophical context. Vergil's rationis egentem
indicates that only a rational hero can prevail over the Hydra's dangers.
So Hercules emerges even more clearly as a worthy model for Aeneas.

The bard's song telescopes present and future, individual and cosmic
destinies, into a single moment of poetic vision. The alignment of human
events with universal processes for mutual illumination is perfectly evi-
denced in Iopas' song where the broader, Platonic mysteries, are glimpsed
in this prelude to human tragedy. Ferguson (42) offers further comment
on the controlling images of fire and wound inAen. 4. Only the hearth-fire,
token of domesticity and family life, is missing. The wound has two
tangential aspects: (1) disease (fire and poison), and (2) the hunt (hunter
and quarry). Bertman's article (46) explores the polarity of generations
in Aen. 5, where the generation gap is given symbolic, forceful exposition.
Opperman (47) and Somville (48) provide critical studies of the Palinurus
episode in Aen. 5. Both concentrate on the Somnus passage, the implica-
tions of the intrusion and the response of the helmsman. Stégen (49)
compares and contrasts the Vergilian boxing match with Homer, Iliad,
23, 653-75, the obvious model, but with important structural differences.
Barbu (50) deals with the hierarchy of human values and the law of univer-
sal necessity discernible in Aen. 6. Dupont-Neraudau (52) combine to
give some novel insights into the Marcellus episode. Rieks (53) and Rut-
ledge (54) are complementary exercises; Rieks is concerned with the
heroic pomp at the close, Rutledge with the Daedalic gallery at the outset
of Aen. 6 Rutledge argues that the artist is a creature of marked insight,
with interpretative gifts which can impart order, direction and definition
to men's lives; Aeneas studies the panels with a sense of their importance.
The labyrinth suggests the ordeal and the triumph which await Aeneas
in the underworld. Sabbadini's (5) Aeneid 6, with commentary, is a wel-

9 (1970) 187-98. He provides superb photographs of the area which made
a profound impression on the poet and on modern Vergilians who follow
in his steps. Solmsen (58) argues against the tripartite scheme of Aen.
6, detected by Norden, Frances Norwood, and Otis, as irrelevant and

title: The bard's song telescopes present and future, individual and cosmic destinies, into a single moment of poetic vision. The alignment of human events with universal processes for mutual illumination is perfectly evidenced in Iopas' song where the broader, Platonic mysteries, are glimpsed in this prelude to human tragedy. But the prime motivation to epic composition was educational. Tracy (97) concentrates on omens and divination in Vergil's opera and argues that they are not to be viewed as a matter of literary or political etiquette, but as a positive directional force. Their prevalence in Aen. 1-6 diminishes in Aen. 7-12, where the colours are brighter and the initiative is human. Aen. 6 may be viewed as an avowal of Vergil's desertion of Epicurus for modified Stoicism with its concomitant doctrine of Providence and divine concern for human affairs. Van Wees (98) examines geographical names as stylistic ornament, finds several categories of their usage, and extols them as marks of scholarship and important insights into the poet's desire for euphony, and into the range of his associational thinking
